text: The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection using monopolar curved scissors, retracting the sigmoid and descending colon with a tip-up grasper and fenestrated bipolar graspers. Adhesions from previous surgeries are divided, and the left ureter is identified and preserved. Dissection continues through the left paracolic gutter, mobilizing the descending colon and further mobilizing the upper rectum.

The surgeon encounters evidence of diverticulitis and divides adhesions from the diseased sigmoid colon to the bladder and pelvic sidewall. The distal transection site is marked in the mesorectum, and the proximal transection site is marked in the disease-free colon. Resection proceeds along the sigmoid mesentery close to the colon using a vessel sealer, preserving the inferior mesenteric artery and superior rectal artery.

The surgeon transects the upper rectum using a vessel sealer, avoiding diathermy to prevent thermal injury near the anastomosis site. A PDS endo-loop is secured to the distal aspect of the specimen for easier retrieval. The proximal bowel is transected along a healthy portion of the descending colon. A small soft Alexis retractor is introduced via the rectum to smooth the extraction pathway and protect the rectal cuff. The specimen is retrieved by grasping the secured endo-loop.

The surgeon performs a double per-string stapled anastomosis using a 33mm circular stapler. The anvil is passed transanally and secured in the colon with a 3-0 V-lock suture, while a PDS endo loop snugly fits the colon around the anvil spike. The Alexis retractor is removed with a cocker, and a purse string suture is placed in the distal rectum with the stapler pistol in situ.

The surgeon holds the stapler in place with a drape clip to facilitate the placement of the distal purse string using a two OV lock suture. The stapler anvil is engaged with the pistol, and correct orientation is confirmed. Indocyanine green is injected intravenously, and vascularity is verified using firefly mode.

The surgeon reinforces the anastomosis using interrupted three-vicryl sutures, ensuring the mesentery is freed medially for a tension-free connection. An insufflation test is performed with a colonoscope to check for leaks at the anastomosis. The eight-millimeter port sites below the umbilicus are closed with transvascular ovicryl, and the skin is sealed with hysteroacryl glue.
